output.final_deliverable:
Research results and synthesis

Overview of actions performed
1) Visited: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps — RAW VISIT CONTENT provided and used as primary evidence.
2) Admiralty law (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law) — RAW VISIT CONTENT was NOT provided in the data; only retrieved-memory snippets and link lists were available. Because the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT for this page is missing, I cannot supply verbatim quotes from that Wikipedia page; I report exactly what evidence is available below and identify the missing evidence.
3) Mathematics of paper folding (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding) — RAW VISIT CONTENT was NOT provided in the data; only retrieved-memory snippets and link lists were available. Because the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT for that page is missing, I cannot supply verbatim quotes from that Wikipedia page; I report exactly what evidence is available below and identify the missing evidence.

Section A — Cordyceps (from RAW VISIT CONTENT at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps)
Requested facts:
(a) The common name for the behaviour Cordyceps induces in ants
- Evidence (verbatim quote from the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT): According to the Cordyceps Wikipedia page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps): "Scientific American notes that some species in the genus 'are indeed body snatchers–they have been making real zombies for millions of years', though of ants or tarantulas, not of humans." This quote shows the page describes the manipulated behaviour as "zombies" (commonly called "zombie" or "zombie-ant" behaviour). [Quote verbatim: "are indeed body snatchers–they have been making real zombies for millions of years"]
- Extracted fact (explicit): The common-name description used on the page for the behaviour induced in ants is "zombies" (often referred to in popular literature as "zombie-ant" behaviour). Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps

(b) One commercial or medicinal use of Cordyceps
- Evidence (verbatim quotes from the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT):
  - "Diverse variants of cordyceps have had more than 1,500 years of use in Chinese medicine." (from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps)
  - "Cordyceps sensu lato (which now includes Ophiocordyceps and many other genera holding species originally in this genus) has long been used in traditional Chinese medicine in the belief it can be used to treat diseases." (from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps)
- Extracted fact (explicit): Cordyceps have a long history of use in traditional Chinese medicine (a commercial/medicinal use), with variants being used for more than 1,500 years. Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps

Section B — Admiralty law (requested: (a) historical origin/earliest legal code associated with admiralty law, (b) one modern international maritime convention or treaty)
- Important procedural note: The RAW_VISIT_CONTENT for the Admiralty law Wikipedia page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law) was NOT included in the provided RAW VISIT CONTENT. The data set contains retrieval-memory entries and link lists referring to that page, but not the page's full raw text. The developer instructions require that for visited pages I extract facts from RAW_VISIT_CONTENT and include verbatim quotes. Because that RAW page text is missing, I cannot produce verbatim quotations from the Admiralty law page. Below I report the exact available evidence and what is therefore missing.

Available retrieved-memory evidence and links (from the provided data):
- The retrieved-memory entries include links and mentions connecting Admiralty law to ancient legal codes, specifically the Code of Hammurabi and the Corpus Juris Civilis. (See entries in the RETRIEVED MEMORIES: items listing "Code_of_Hammurabi" and "Corpus_Juris_Civilis" as links associated with the Admiralty law page. The Admiralty law page URL in the memory: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law.)
- The retrieved-memory link list also shows the presence of many related linked pages (for example, Carriage_of_Goods_by_Sea_Act appears among linked pages in the memory snapshot).

What is missing (explicit):
- I do NOT have the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT text of https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law in the provided data, so I cannot quote verbatim phrases from that specific Wikipedia page. Because the raw page content is missing, I cannot definitively extract and quote the exact phrasing on that page for (a) or (b).

Provisional facts supported by retrieved-memory links (not RAW verbatim quotes — stated as such):
- Historical origin / earliest legal code associated with admiralty law: Retrieved-memory evidence lists the Code of Hammurabi and the Corpus Juris Civilis among links associated with the Admiralty law page, indicating those ancient legal codes are referenced on or connected to the page. Source (retrieved-memory pointing to the page): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law
- One modern maritime instrument referenced in the retrieved-memory link list: Carriage_of_Goods_by_Sea_Act is visible among related links in the retrieved-memory snapshot for Admiralty law (this indicates the Admiralty law page links to modern legislative or treaty topics such as carriage-of-goods law). Source (retrieved-memory): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law

Caveat and recommended next step: If you require verbatim quotes and fully-verified extractions from the Admiralty law page itself (as the mandate specified), I need the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT for https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law (i.e., I must actually visit that page and capture its raw text). I can fetch and extract the requested verbatim quotes on demand.

Section C — Mathematics of paper folding (requested: (a) the name of one theorem or axiom related to origami mathematics, (b) one practical engineering application of origami folding)
- Important procedural note: The RAW_VISIT_CONTENT for the Mathematics of paper folding page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding) was NOT included in the provided RAW VISIT CONTENT. The data set contains retrieved-memory entries and link lists referring to that page, but not the page's full raw text. Because the RAW page content is missing, I cannot produce verbatim quotations from that Wikipedia page. I report what evidence is available and what is missing.

Available retrieved-memory evidence and links (from the provided data):
- The retrieved-memory entries reference "Overview of Origami Axioms" and point to origami axioms resources (these items in memory point to pages discussing origami axioms such as the Huzita–Hatori axioms). The memory also includes references to topics like "Trisecting an arbitrary angle" and "Geometric Origami." The specific memory notes: "Overview of Origami Axioms" (link) and mentions of 'universality results', 'efficient decision algorithms', and 'engineering' interest from Stanford, Caltech, and MIT in origami contests/research.
- The retrieved-memory makes clear that the Mathematics of paper folding page covers origami axioms and engineering applications (e.g., foldability problems, universality results) and that computational origami research connects to real engineering design.

What is missing (explicit):
- I do NOT have the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT text of https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding in the provided data, so I cannot quote verbatim phrases from that specific Wikipedia page. Because the raw page content is missing, I cannot definitively extract and quote the exact phrasing on that page for (a) or (b).

Provisional facts supported by retrieved-memory links (not RAW verbatim quotes — stated as such):
(a) A named axiom/theorem related to origami mathematics: The retrieved-memory references "Overview of Origami Axioms" and related materials; the canonical set of origami axioms commonly discussed in such contexts are the Huzita–Hatori axioms (the memory points to origami-axiom resources). Source (retrieved-memory pointing to relevant material and the page): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding
(b) One practical engineering application of origami folding: The retrieved-memory indicates the page discusses engineering interest and applications such as deployable structures and foldability in engineering design (research involvement from Stanford, Caltech, MIT is mentioned in the memory). These engineering applications frequently include deployable structures (e.g., deployable solar arrays, stents, foldable shelters) although the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT is not present here to quote specific examples on the Wikipedia page. Source (retrieved-memory pointing to the page): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding

Caveat and recommended next step: If you require verbatim quotes directly from the Wikipedia page for Mathematics of paper folding (as the mandate required), I need to fetch the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT from that page and will then provide precise verbatim quotes and exact extracted facts.

Section D — 150+ word synthesis essay identifying a UNIFYING THEME that connects all three topics (must reference specific facts from each topic; each fact should have a Wikipedia URL citation). Note on evidence use: For Cordyceps I use verbatim RAW_VISIT_CONTENT quotes (available). For Admiralty law and Mathematics of paper folding I reference the retrieved-memory evidence and the page URLs but cannot provide verbatim quotes because the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT for those pages was not included in the provided data. I explicitly indicate where statements are based on the Cordyceps RAW_VISIT_CONTENT and where they are based on retrieved-memory evidence.

Synthesis essay (UNIFYING THEME: "Constrained rules producing complex, emergent function")

Across these three, a single pattern stands out: constrained rules or structures — whether biological, legal, or geometric — give rise to complex, emergent functions that are leveraged by life and by people. In the fungal world, Cordyceps species manipulate insect hosts in highly constrained ways to complete a life cycle; the Cordyceps page explicitly characterizes that manipulation in popular terms, noting that "Scientific American notes that some species in the genus 'are indeed body snatchers–they have been making real zombies for millions of years'" (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps). Cordyceps are also embedded in long-standing human practices: "Diverse variants of cordyceps have had more than 1,500 years of use in Chinese medicine" (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps). In law, Admiralty law grew from ancient legal codes and has evolved modern international instruments to regulate highly constrained maritime activity; the retrieved-memory evidence for the Admiralty law page shows links to ancient legal codes such as the Code of Hammurabi and the Corpus Juris Civilis and to modern carriage-of-goods law topics (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law). These ancient and modern legal frameworks impose structured rules on maritime conduct and thereby enable complex global shipping systems to function. In mathematics and engineering, the Mathematics of paper folding exposes how a small set of folding axioms (retrieved-memory references point to origami axioms such as the Huzita–Hatori axioms) can generate a vast range of shapes; the retrieved-memory evidence for the page notes 'origami axioms' and engineering foldability research at institutions like Stanford and Caltech, and points to engineering applications (e.g., deployable structures) that exploit those constrained folding rules (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding). Together the three topics illustrate the same idea: simple, local rules or structures (a fungus's infection program; ancient and modern maritime codes; origami axioms and fold patterns) enable sophisticated, large-scale behaviors — from manipulated ant actions and traditional medicinal uses to regulated global navigation and engineered deployable devices. Each domain shows how constraint + repeated application = emergent complexity (Cordyceps: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps; Admiralty law (retrieved-memory links): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law; Mathematics of paper folding (retrieved-memory links): https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding).

Concluding note and offer
- Concluding note: I was able to extract the Cordyceps facts with verbatim RAW_VISIT_CONTENT quotes and full citation (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps). For Admiralty law and Mathematics of paper folding, the provided data set did not include the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT for those pages, only retrieved-memory entries and link lists; therefore I could not supply verbatim quotes from those two Wikipedia pages. I have reported the retrieved-memory evidence and the page URLs for those topics. If you would like, I can now visit the two missing Wikipedia pages directly (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law and https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding), extract the exact requested facts, and provide verbatim quotes from their RAW_VISIT_CONTENT as required. Would you like me to fetch those two pages and complete the missing verbatim quotations and exact extractions?
output.action_summary: I visited and extracted facts (with verbatim quotes) from the Cordyceps Wikipedia page: (a) the page describes the manipulated behaviour as 'zombies' — "are indeed body snatchers–they have been making real zombies for millions of years" — and (b) Cordyceps variants have been used in Chinese medicine for more than 1,500 years (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps). For the Admiralty law and Mathematics of paper folding pages, the RAW_VISIT_CONTENT was not provided in the data; only retrieved-memory link snippets were available. From those memories I report that Admiralty law links to ancient legal codes (e.g., Code of Hammurabi, Corpus Juris Civilis) and modern carriage-of-goods law topics (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law), and that the mathematics-of-paper-folding page references origami axioms (e.g., Huzita–Hatori axioms) and engineering applications such as deployable structures (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding). I provided a 150+ word essay tying the three topics under the unifying theme 'constrained rules producing complex, emergent function.' If you want verbatim quotes and fully verified extractions from the two missing pages, I can fetch their RAW_VISIT_CONTENT now and update the answer.

2bbeb086-6457-4eda-82ef-a1fe50d2e995.title:
Research three unrelated niche topics, extract the requested facts from each Wikipedia page, and then write a synthesis.

1. Cordyceps fungi: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps — find (a) the common name for the behaviour Cordyceps induces in ants, (b) one commercial or medicinal use of Cordyceps.

2. Admiralty law: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law — find (a) the historical origin or earliest legal code associated with admiralty law, (b) one modern international maritime convention or treaty.

3. Mathematics of paper folding: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding — find (a) the name of one theorem or axiom related to origami mathematics, (b) one practical engineering application of origami folding.

Finally, write a 150+ word essay identifying a UNIFYING THEME that connects all three topics (e.g., hidden complexity, emergent structure, nature inspiring engineering). The essay must reference specific facts from each topic. Cite the Wikipedia URL for every fact.

action_result.content:
Cordyceps
Cordyceps militaris
Scientific classification
Kingdom:
Fungi
Division:
Ascomycota
Class:
Sordariomycetes
Order:
Hypocreales
Family:
Cordycipitaceae
Genus:
Cordyceps
Fr.
(1818)
Type species
Cordyceps militaris
(
L.
) Fr. (1818)
Synonyms
List
Akrophyton
Lebert (1858)
Alphitomyces
Reissek (1856)
Amphichorda
Fr. (1825)
Campylothecium
Ces. (1846)
Cordylia
Fr. (1818)
Cordyliceps
Fr. (1832)
Coremiopsis
Sizova & Suprun (1957)
Corynesphaera
Dumort.2 (1822)
Evlachovaea
B.A. Borisov & Tarasov (1999)
Hypoxylum
Juss. (1789)
Isaria
Pers. (1794)
Phytocordyceps
C.H. Su & H.H. Wang (1986)
Polistophthora
Lebert (1858)
Racemella
Ces. (1861)
Ramaria
Holmsk. (1781)
Tettigorhyza
G. Bertol. (1875)
Torrubia
Lév. (1863)
Xylaria
Hill ex Grev (1823)
Cordyceps
/
ˈ
k
ɔːr
d
ɪ
s
ɛ
p
s
/
is a
genus
of
ascomycete
fungi
(sac fungi) that includes over 260 species worldwide, many of which are parasitic. Diverse variants of cordyceps have had more than 1,500 years of use in
Chinese medicine
.
Most
Cordyceps
species are
endoparasitoids
, parasitic mainly on
insects
and other
arthropods
(they are thus
entomopathogenic fungi
); a few are parasitic on other fungi.
The generic name
Cordyceps
is derived from the
ancient Greek
κορδύλη
kordýlē
, meaning "club", and the
Latin
-ceps
, derived from Latin
caput
, meaning "head".
The
genus
has a worldwide distribution, with most of the known species
being from Asia.
Taxonomy
There are two recognized
subgenera
:
Cordyceps
subgen.
Cordyceps
Fr. 1818
Cordyceps
subgen.
Cordylia
Tul.
&
C. Tul.
1865
Cordyceps
sensu stricto
are the
teleomorphs
of several
genera
of
anamorphic
,
entomopathogenic fungi
such as
Beauveria
(
Cordyceps bassiana
),
Septofusidium
, and
Lecanicillium
.
Splits
Cordyceps
subgen.
Epichloe
was at one time a subgenus, but is now regarded as a separate genus,
Epichloë
.
Cordyceps
subgen.
Ophiocordyceps
was at one time a subgenus defined by morphology.
Nuclear DNA
sampling done in 2007 shows that members, including "
C. sinensis
" and "
C. unilateralis
", as well as some others not placed in the subgenus, were distantly related to most of the remainder of species then placed in
Cordyceps
(e.g. the type species
C. militaris
). As a result, it became its own genus, absorbing new members.
The 2007 study also peeled off
Metacordyceps
(anamorph
Metarhizium
,
Pochonia
) and
Elaphocordyceps
. A number of species remain unclearly assigned and provisionally retained in
Cordyceps
sensu lato
.
Selected species
Main article:
List of Cordyceps species
There are over 260 species in the genus
Cordyceps
including the following species:
Cordyceps caespitosa
Cordyceps chanhua
Cordyceps militaris
Cordyceps sinclairii
Anamorphic genera
Isaria
is a genus name that has been applied to many anamorphs of
Cordyceps
species. This genus itself is treated as a synonym of
Cordyceps
in Species Fungorum following the "one fungus one name" change,
but many species names with
Isaria
are still preferred by Species Fungorum over the synonyms in other genera (e.g.
Isaria sinclarii
is preferred over
Cordyceps sinclairii
).
Though confusing, this does match the "equal footing for priority" approach of the "one fungus one name" concept. To add to the complexity,
Isaria
is a
conserved name
with a conserved type.
What remains under
Isaria
as of 2016 remains polyphyletic and can be divided into three main clades.
Anamorphic genera closely allied to
Cordyceps
sensu stricto include
Evlachovaea
,
Lecanicillium
and
Beauveria
.
Biology
When
Cordyceps
attacks a host, the
mycelium
invades and eventually replaces the host tissue, while the elongated fruit body (
ascocarp
) may be cylindrical, branched, or of complex shape. The ascocarp bears many small, flask-shaped
perithecia
containing
asci
. These, in turn, contain thread-like
ascospores
, which usually break into fragments and are presumably infective.
Cordyceps
are used by
drab stinkbugs
to protect their eggs from
parasitoid wasps
.
Research
Polysaccharide
components and the nucleoside
cordycepin
isolated from
C. militaris
are under
basic research
, but more advanced
clinical research
has been limited and too low in quality to identify any therapeutic potential of cordyceps components.
Uses
Cordyceps
sensu lato
(which now includes
Ophiocordyceps
and many other genera holding species originally in this genus) has long been used in
traditional Chinese medicine
in the belief it can be used to treat diseases.
There is no
scientific
evidence for such uses.
Cultural representations
The video game series
The Last of Us
(2013–2021) and its
television adaptation
present
Cordyceps
as a deadly threat to the human race, its parasitism powerful enough to result in global calamity.
The result is a
zombie apocalypse
and the collapse of human civilization.
Scientific American
notes that some species in the genus "are indeed body snatchers–they have been making real zombies for millions of years", though of ants or
tarantulas
, not of humans.
The Last of Us
proceeds from the premise that a new species of
Cordyceps
manages to
jump the species barrier
, from nonhuman to human, as diseases like influenza and viruses like
Ebola
and
COVID-19
have done. Its human hosts initially become violent "infected" beings, before turning into blind zombie "clickers", with fungal growths emerging from their faces.
In a detail that reflects
Cordyceps
biology, "clickers" then seek out a dark place in which to die and release the fungal spores, enabling the parasite to complete its life cycle.
Scientific American
comments that by combining a plausible mechanism with effective artistic design, the series gains "both scientific rigor and beauty".
In similar vein,
Cordyceps
causes a pandemic that wipes out most of humanity in
Mike Carey
's 2014 postapocalyptic novel
The Girl with All the Gifts
and its 2016
film adaptation
.
In this case, an infected person becomes a "hungry", a zombie thirsting for blood. In the fiction, Dr. Caldwell explains that the human-infecting fungus is a mutated form of
Ophiocordyceps unilateralis
(a group of species now split off from
Cordyceps
) which alters the behaviour of infected insects. The children of infected mothers, however, become "hybrids" with antibodies protecting them against the fungus.
Gallery

Cordyceps militaris
Cordyceps ophioglossoides

Medicinal fungi

action_result.content_full:
Cordyceps
Cordyceps militaris
Scientific classification
Kingdom:
Fungi
Division:
Ascomycota
Class:
Sordariomycetes
Order:
Hypocreales
Family:
Cordycipitaceae
Genus:
Cordyceps
Fr.
(1818)
Type species
Cordyceps militaris
(
L.
) Fr. (1818)
Synonyms
List
Akrophyton
Lebert (1858)
Alphitomyces
Reissek (1856)
Amphichorda
Fr. (1825)
Campylothecium
Ces. (1846)
Cordylia
Fr. (1818)
Cordyliceps
Fr. (1832)
Coremiopsis
Sizova & Suprun (1957)
Corynesphaera
Dumort.2 (1822)
Evlachovaea
B.A. Borisov & Tarasov (1999)
Hypoxylum
Juss. (1789)
Isaria
Pers. (1794)
Phytocordyceps
C.H. Su & H.H. Wang (1986)
Polistophthora
Lebert (1858)
Racemella
Ces. (1861)
Ramaria
Holmsk. (1781)
Tettigorhyza
G. Bertol. (1875)
Torrubia
Lév. (1863)
Xylaria
Hill ex Grev (1823)
Cordyceps
/
ˈ
k
ɔːr
d
ɪ
s
ɛ
p
s
/
is a
genus
of
ascomycete
fungi
(sac fungi) that includes over 260 species worldwide, many of which are parasitic. Diverse variants of cordyceps have had more than 1,500 years of use in
Chinese medicine
.
Most
Cordyceps
species are
endoparasitoids
, parasitic mainly on
insects
and other
arthropods
(they are thus
entomopathogenic fungi
); a few are parasitic on other fungi.
The generic name
Cordyceps
is derived from the
ancient Greek
κορδύλη
kordýlē
, meaning "club", and the
Latin
-ceps
, derived from Latin
caput
, meaning "head".
The
genus
has a worldwide distribution, with most of the known species
being from Asia.
Taxonomy
There are two recognized
subgenera
:
Cordyceps
subgen.
Cordyceps
Fr. 1818
Cordyceps
subgen.
Cordylia
Tul.
&
C. Tul.
1865
Cordyceps
sensu stricto
are the
teleomorphs
of several
genera
of
anamorphic
,
entomopathogenic fungi
such as
Beauveria
(
Cordyceps bassiana
),
Septofusidium
, and
Lecanicillium
.
Splits
Cordyceps
subgen.
Epichloe
was at one time a subgenus, but is now regarded as a separate genus,
Epichloë
.
Cordyceps
subgen.
Ophiocordyceps
was at one time a subgenus defined by morphology.
Nuclear DNA
sampling done in 2007 shows that members, including "
C. sinensis
" and "
C. unilateralis
", as well as some others not placed in the subgenus, were distantly related to most of the remainder of species then placed in
Cordyceps
(e.g. the type species
C. militaris
). As a result, it became its own genus, absorbing new members.
The 2007 study also peeled off
Metacordyceps
(anamorph
Metarhizium
,
Pochonia
) and
Elaphocordyceps
. A number of species remain unclearly assigned and provisionally retained in
Cordyceps
sensu lato
.
Selected species
Main article:
List of Cordyceps species
There are over 260 species in the genus
Cordyceps
including the following species:
Cordyceps caespitosa
Cordyceps chanhua
Cordyceps militaris
Cordyceps sinclairii
Anamorphic genera
Isaria
is a genus name that has been applied to many anamorphs of
Cordyceps
species. This genus itself is treated as a synonym of
Cordyceps
in Species Fungorum following the "one fungus one name" change,
but many species names with
Isaria
are still preferred by Species Fungorum over the synonyms in other genera (e.g.
Isaria sinclarii
is preferred over
Cordyceps sinclairii
).
Though confusing, this does match the "equal footing for priority" approach of the "one fungus one name" concept. To add to the complexity,
Isaria
is a
conserved name
with a conserved type.
What remains under
Isaria
as of 2016 remains polyphyletic and can be divided into three main clades.
Anamorphic genera closely allied to
Cordyceps
sensu stricto include
Evlachovaea
,
Lecanicillium
and
Beauveria
.
Biology
When
Cordyceps
attacks a host, the
mycelium
invades and eventually replaces the host tissue, while the elongated fruit body (
ascocarp
) may be cylindrical, branched, or of complex shape. The ascocarp bears many small, flask-shaped
perithecia
containing
asci
. These, in turn, contain thread-like
ascospores
, which usually break into fragments and are presumably infective.
Cordyceps
are used by
drab stinkbugs
to protect their eggs from
parasitoid wasps
.
Research
Polysaccharide
components and the nucleoside
cordycepin
isolated from
C. militaris
are under
basic research
, but more advanced
clinical research
has been limited and too low in quality to identify any therapeutic potential of cordyceps components.
Uses
Cordyceps
sensu lato
(which now includes
Ophiocordyceps
and many other genera holding species originally in this genus) has long been used in
traditional Chinese medicine
in the belief it can be used to treat diseases.
There is no
scientific
evidence for such uses.
Cultural representations
The video game series
The Last of Us
(2013–2021) and its
television adaptation
present
Cordyceps
as a deadly threat to the human race, its parasitism powerful enough to result in global calamity.
The result is a
zombie apocalypse
and the collapse of human civilization.
Scientific American
notes that some species in the genus "are indeed body snatchers–they have been making real zombies for millions of years", though of ants or
tarantulas
, not of humans.
The Last of Us
proceeds from the premise that a new species of
Cordyceps
manages to
jump the species barrier
, from nonhuman to human, as diseases like influenza and viruses like
Ebola
and
COVID-19
have done. Its human hosts initially become violent "infected" beings, before turning into blind zombie "clickers", with fungal growths emerging from their faces.
In a detail that reflects
Cordyceps
biology, "clickers" then seek out a dark place in which to die and release the fungal spores, enabling the parasite to complete its life cycle.
Scientific American
comments that by combining a plausible mechanism with effective artistic design, the series gains "both scientific rigor and beauty".
In similar vein,
Cordyceps
causes a pandemic that wipes out most of humanity in
Mike Carey
's 2014 postapocalyptic novel
The Girl with All the Gifts
and its 2016
film adaptation
.
In this case, an infected person becomes a "hungry", a zombie thirsting for blood. In the fiction, Dr. Caldwell explains that the human-infecting fungus is a mutated form of
Ophiocordyceps unilateralis
(a group of species now split off from
Cordyceps
) which alters the behaviour of infected insects. The children of infected mothers, however, become "hybrids" with antibodies protecting them against the fungus.
Gallery
Cordyceps militaris
Cordyceps militaris
Cordyceps ophioglossoides
See also
Medicinal fungi

.
Journal of Ethnopharmacology
.
96
(
1–
2):
19–
29.

10.1016/j.jep.2004.09.029
.

15588646
.

4af390eb-cb15-41ef-9969-0d797716801d.title: Visit Admiralty law Wikipedia page to extract requested facts

details.extraction_intent: From the Admiralty law Wikipedia article, extract (a) the historical origin or earliest legal code associated with admiralty law, and (b) one modern international maritime convention or treaty mentioned on the page.
details.measurable_acceptance_criteria: Provide the name and short description/quote of the historical origin or earliest legal code cited on the page, and the name of a modern convention or treaty mentioned on the page, plus the page URL.
details.justification: The explicit URL was given; visiting it ensures accurate extraction of the earliest legal code reference and a modern treaty for citation in the synthesis.
details.goal: Visit Admiralty law Wikipedia page to extract requested facts
details.original_goal: Visit Admiralty law Wikipedia page to extract requested facts
details.parent_goal:
Research three unrelated niche topics, extract the requested facts from each Wikipedia page, and then write a synthesis.

1. Cordyceps fungi: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps — find (a) the common name for the behaviour Cordyceps induces in ants, (b) one commercial or medicinal use of Cordyceps.

2. Admiralty law: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law — find (a) the historical origin or earliest legal code associated with admiralty law, (b) one modern international maritime convention or treaty.

3. Mathematics of paper folding: visit https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding — find (a) the name of one theorem or axiom related to origami mathematics, (b) one practical engineering application of origami folding.

Finally, write a 150+ word essay identifying a UNIFYING THEME that connects all three topics (e.g., hidden complexity, emergent structure, nature inspiring engineering). The essay must reference specific facts from each topic. Cite the Wikipedia URL for every fact.

3fa942be-9025-474b-a3e3-4ef71b0257d5.title: Visit Mathematics of paper folding Wikipedia page to extract requested facts

details.extraction_intent: From the Mathematics of paper folding article, extract (a) the name of one theorem or axiom related to origami mathematics (e.g., an origami axiom name), and (b) one practical engineering application of origami folding described on the page.
details.measurable_acceptance_criteria: Provide the theorem/axiom name exactly as on the page and a brief quoted description of one engineering application, plus the page URL.
details.justification: Visiting the specified page yields the authoritative names and applications needed to support the final thematic essay and citations.

Finally, write a 150+ word essay identifying a UNIFYING THEME that connects all three topics (e.g., hidden complexity, emergent structure, nature inspiring engineering). The essay must reference specific facts from each topic. Cite the Wikipedia URL for every fact.

b0796e7d-1780-4dad-ae2f-aa2611c5283f.title: Write synthesis essay (use extracted facts and cite each Wikipedia URL)

details.task: Using the extracted fields from the three visit actions, write a synthesis essay of at least 150 words identifying a single UNIFYING THEME that connects Cordyceps fungi, Admiralty law, and mathematics of paper folding. The essay must (1) reference specific extracted facts from each topic (use the exact extracted phrases where possible), and (2) cite the exact Wikipedia URL for every fact (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cordyceps, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding).
output_fields: inline_citations (mapping of facts to source URLs)
details.measurable_acceptance_criteria: Essay length >=150 words; includes at least one explicit fact from each topic and cites the corresponding Wikipedia URL for each fact.
details.justification: A think step composes the final synthesis using verified facts from the three visited pages; it depends on those visits' outputs and will produce the required essay and citations.
details.goal: Write synthesis essay (use extracted facts and cite each Wikipedia URL)
details.original_goal: Write synthesis essay (use extracted facts and cite each Wikipedia URL)

think_nodes.title: Write synthesis essay (use extracted facts and cite each Wikipedia URL)

reasons: Cordyceps: Both requested facts were provided with verbatim quotes and the correct Wikipedia URL (common-name behaviour 'zombies' / 'zombie-ant'; medicinal use in traditional Chinese medicine >1,500 years).

Admiralty law: The agent did not provide RAW_VISIT_CONTENT or verbatim quotes from the specified Admiralty law page. Only retrieved-memory/link hints were reported (Code of Hammurabi, Corpus Juris Civilis, Carriage_of_Goods_by_Sea_Act). These do not satisfy the requirement to extract and quote the requested facts directly from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Admiralty_law, nor do they clearly provide a modern international maritime convention/treaty name.

Mathematics of paper folding: The agent's response was truncated and did not supply the requested facts (no theorem/axiom named, no engineering application given), nor any RAW_VISIT_CONTENT from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematics_of_paper_folding.
Synthesis essay: The required 150+ word essay connecting all three topics, citing specific facts and Wikipedia URLs for each fact, was not provided (output cut off before completion).
Because only 2 of 6 requested facts were correctly and verifiably provided and the synthesis requirement was unmet, the task is not passed.
